ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken soup' class (FOODON_03311434); no specific Samgyetang / Korean ginseng chicken soup class exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Chicken soup) is the closest defensible match. No specific Samgyetang code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Samgyetang or Korean ginseng chicken soup identified in the SNOMED CT international release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Korean Ginseng Chicken Soup is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a whole small chicken or chicken pieces submerged in clear herbal broth in a stone or ceramic bowl.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken soup, home-prepared (SR Legacy ID 06172) adapted for Samgyetang composition
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Samgyetang entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken soup (ID 06172) with adjustments for the higher protein content from whole-chicken slow-simmering and the low carbohydrate contribution of ginseng and TCM herbs. Sodium reflects moderate home-preparation levels. All values are estimates and should be treated as medium-confidence approximations.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — analogous estimate for chicken-based broth soup with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Samgyetang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate chicken broth soups and protein-dominant meat soups documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 5.25) / 100 ≈ 1.8. Carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 1.5 × 3.5 = 5.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg per 100 g; ~420 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Korean Ginseng Chicken Soup prepared without added salt provides a moderate sodium load of approximately 420 mg per serving; individuals with hypertension should avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce and should monitor total daily sodium intake to remain within the recommended 1500–2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 35 and only ~5 g of carbohydrate per 350 g serving, Korean Ginseng Chicken Soup produces a minimal glycemic load of approximately 1.8 and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.1 g per 100 g), high-quality lean protein, and bioactive ginseng compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Korean Ginseng Chicken Soup is relatively low in saturated fat and provides lean protein from chicken; Panax ginseng ginsenosides have been associated with modest cardioprotective effects in peer-reviewed literature, making this soup a heart-friendly option when prepared without excess salt or fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory bioactive compounds from ginseng and collagen-rich chicken broth
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The ginsenosides in Panax ginseng and the collagen peptides released during slow-simmering of chicken may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefits; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may include this soup as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Ginseng Chicken Soup, known in Korean as Samgyetang (삼계탕) and in Mandarin as Rénshēn Jītāng (人参鸡汤 / 人參雞湯), is a traditional restorative soup originating from Korea with parallel preparations in Chinese medicinal cuisine. The dish is prepared by slow-simmering or double-steaming a whole small chicken — typically stuffed with glutinous rice, garlic, and jujube dates — together with high-quality Panax ginseng root, goji berries, and dried yam in water for two to four hours. The result is a clear, golden broth with a distinctive mildly bitter, earthy, and subtly sweet herbal flavour profile. The chicken becomes extremely tender and falls off the bone, while the glutinous rice absorbs the broth and softens completely. Nutritionally, the soup is high in lean protein (~8.5 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (~1.5 g per 100 g), and moderate in fat (~3.8 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 75 kcal per 100 g. It has an estimated low glycemic index of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per 350 g serving, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking blood sugar control. The sodium content of approximately 120 mg per 100 g is moderate and manageable for individuals with hypertension when prepared without excess added salt. Ginseng ginsenosides have been studied for potential immunomodulatory, anti-fatigue, and cardioprotective properties. The soup is considered halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is traditionally consumed during summer in Korea as a restorative meal to combat heat fatigue, and is also popular as a recovery food after illness or physical exertion.
common_user_queries: Is Korean ginseng chicken soup healthy?
How many calories in ginseng chicken soup?
Is ginseng chicken soup good for diabetics?
What are the benefits of Samgyetang?
How is Korean ginseng chicken soup made?
Is ginseng chicken soup low carb?
Can I eat ginseng chicken soup if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact Samgyetang entry was found in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken soup (ID 06172) with adjustments for the whole-chicken slow-simmering preparation and TCM herb additions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken soup (ID 06172); no exact Samgyetang entry exists in any approved primary source as of April 2024. Values should be validated against a laboratory analysis of a standardised Samgyetang recipe when available. 2. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture content for slow-simmered Samgyetang is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace for home-prepared chicken soup per USDA SR Legacy data. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Samgyetang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input record listed a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (350g)' entries); only one default serving of 350 g is retained. 6. The input source cited ('Journal of Ginseng Research') is not on the approved source list for nutrient values and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient source; the ginseng research journal is retained as contextual support for health suitability notes only. 7. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for Samgyetang exists.
